introduction: Calendar problems ask you to determine the day of the week for a given date, check whether a year is a leap year, or find when a calendar repeats itself. The technique relies entirely on counting odd days — the remainder left when the total number of days is divided by 7. Once you master odd-day counting, any calendar question becomes straightforward arithmetic.
explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

An ordinary (non-leap) year has 365 days = 52 weeks + 1 day, so it contributes 1 odd day. A leap year has 366 days = 52 weeks + 2 days, contributing 2 odd days.

A year is a leap year if it is divisible by 4, except century years, which must be divisible by 400 to be leap years (so 1900 is not a leap year, but 2000 is).

For counting odd days over centuries: 100 years contain 5 odd days, 200 years contain 3 odd days, 300 years contain 1 odd day, and 400 years contain 0 odd days (because 400 years have exactly 97 leap years, making the total days a multiple of 7).

To find the day of the week for any date, count the total odd days from a known reference point up to that date, then map the remainder (0 to 6) to a day using the convention: 0 = Sunday, 1 = Monday, 2 = Tuesday, 3 = Wednesday, 4 = Thursday, 5 = Friday, 6 = Saturday.

For a specific date within a year, add the odd days contributed by full months already passed (using each month's day count mod 7) plus the day number itself, then take mod 7 relative to the reference day.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Scheduling recurring events, verifying historical dates, determining if a particular date in the past or future falls on a weekend, and understanding why calendars "repeat" after certain intervals all rely on this odd-day logic.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Forgetting to check the century rule for leap years (divisible by 100 but not 400 is NOT a leap year).
2) Counting the reference date's own day incorrectly when computing days elapsed.
3) Using 365 days for a leap year by mistake.
4) Mixing up the day-code convention (which number maps to which weekday).
5) Not reducing the total odd days modulo 7 before mapping to a weekday.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• Leap year rule: divisible by 4 and (not divisible by 100 or divisible by 400).

• 100 years → 5 odd days; 200 years → 3 odd days; 300 years → 1 odd day; 400 years → 0 odd days.

• A calendar for a non-leap year repeats itself after another year with the same odd-day count and leap status; the same calendar (with same start day) typically recurs after 28 years under simple conditions, though the exact gap depends on the leap year pattern.

• Always double check whether February has 28 or 29 days for the year in question.

formulas.title: Odd days per century block
formulas.content: 100 years = 5 odd days; 200 years = 3 odd days; 300 years = 1 odd day; 400 years = 0 odd days

formulas.title: Leap year test
formulas.content: Year is leap if divisible by 4 AND (not divisible by 100 OR divisible by 400)

shortcutTips.title: Memorise the 400-year cycle
shortcutTips.tip: Every block of exactly 400 years contributes 0 odd days, so the calendar of year Y matches year Y+400 exactly.

shortcutTips.title: Century shortcuts
shortcutTips.tip: Remember 100→5, 200→3, 300→1, 400→0 odd days instead of recomputing leap years each time.

shortcutTips.title: Month odd-day table
shortcutTips.tip: Precompute odd days contributed by each month (31 days = 3 odd days, 30 days = 2 odd days, 28 days = 0, 29 days = 1) to speed up within-year calculations.

shortcutTips.title: Check century leap rule first
shortcutTips.tip: For any year ending in 00, check divisibility by 400 before applying the divisible-by-4 rule.

shortcutTips.title: Use a known anchor
shortcutTips.tip: If you know that 1 January of a certain year falls on a known day, count forward/backward using odd days rather than starting from year 1.

solvedExamples.problem: Is the year 2000 a leap year? Is 1900 a leap year?
solvedExamples.solution: 2000 is a leap year; 1900 is not.
steps: 2000 is divisible by 400, so it is a leap year.
1900 is divisible by 100 but not by 400, so it is not a leap year.

solvedExamples.problem: January 1, 2001 was a Monday. What day of the week was January 1, 2002?
steps: 2001 is not a leap year, so it has 1 odd day.
Adding 1 odd day to Monday moves us to Tuesday.

solvedExamples.problem: Find the number of odd days in 400 years, and explain why the calendar repeats every 400 years.
solvedExamples.solution: 400 years contain exactly 0 odd days, so the calendar repeats.
steps: Odd days in 400 years = odd days in 4 blocks of 100 years combined = 5+5+5+5 = 20, but the 400th year is itself a leap year so an adjustment applies.
Using the standard rule, 400 years contain exactly 97 leap years and 303 ordinary years.
Total days = 303×365 + 97×366 = a number exactly divisible by 7, giving 0 odd days.
Since 0 odd days remain, the weekday pattern realigns exactly after 400 years.
